1: 'Woe to her who is rebellious and defiled, the oppressing city!' The final chapter returns from the foreign nations to Jerusalem itself — the woe oracle now aimed at Jerusalem, identified by its characteristics: rebellious (moreh, refusing instruction), defiled (nig'alah, polluted/unclean), and oppressing (hayonah, literally the dove, but used here in the sense of one who presses/oppresses). The same city that could have been the model of covenant justice is condemned in the same terms as the foreign nations.
2: 'She listens to no voice; she accepts no correction. She does not trust in YHWH; she does not draw near to her God.' Four-fold indictment of Jerusalem's spiritual failure: no listening (lo shame'ah beqol), no accepting correction (lo liqchah musar), no trust in YHWH (lo vatcha baYHWH), no drawing near to God (lo qarevah el-Eloheiha). The four failures correspond to the four means of relationship with YHWH: hearing Torah, receiving prophetic reproof, faith, and worship. Jerusalem has failed in all four dimensions simultaneously.
3: 'Her officials within her are roaring lions; her judges are evening wolves that leave nothing till the morning.' The leadership metaphors: officials as lions (aryayot sho'agim, roaring), judges as evening wolves (ze'evei erev). Evening wolves were those most dangerous at dusk — when they prowled for prey before retiring at dawn. By dawn they had consumed everything. The officials and judges who were appointed to protect the people consume them instead. The predatory leadership imagery pervades the prophets (Micah 3:1-3; Ezekiel 22:27).
4: 'Her prophets are fickle, treacherous men; her priests have profaned what is holy; they have done violence to the law.' The second pair of corrupt offices: prophets (nevi'iha pach'azim anshei begadot, frivolous/reckless treacherous men) and priests (kohaneiha chilelu qodesh chomsu Torah, who profane the holy and do violence to Torah). The priests' role was to maintain the boundaries between holy and common, clean and unclean (Leviticus 10:10; Ezekiel 44:23); they have blurred or abolished those boundaries. Doing violence to Torah (chomsu Torah) means treating the divine instruction as an obstacle to be overcome rather than a boundary to be respected.
5: 'YHWH within her is righteous; he does no injustice; every morning he shows forth his justice; each dawn he does not fail; but the unjust knows no shame.' The contrast with the corrupt leaders: YHWH himself, who dwells within Jerusalem (unlike the leaders who prey on it), is consistently righteous — every morning, at each dawn, his justice appears. The dawn (laboqer... boqer) setting may be the city gate where legal decisions were rendered (Ruth 4:1-2; Amos 5:15). But the unjust (avval) know no shame — the consistent presence of divine righteousness has not moved them to change.
6: 'I have cut off nations; their battlements are in ruins; I have laid waste their streets so that no one walks in them; their cities have been made desolate, without a man, without an inhabitant.' YHWH speaks: the destruction of other nations was a lesson for Jerusalem, but Jerusalem did not learn it. The devastated cities — presumably the nations judged in Zephaniah 2 — serve as visual object lessons of divine power. If YHWH cut off Philistia, Moab, Ammon, Cush, and Assyria, Jerusalem should have understood its own vulnerability. Instead: Zephaniah 3:7.
7: 'I said, Surely you will fear me; you will accept correction. Then your dwelling would not be cut off according to all that I have appointed against you. But all the more they were eager to make all their deeds corrupt.' The divine expectation voiced: Jerusalem should have seen the destruction of the nations and feared. The phrase 'all that I have appointed against you' (kol asher paqadeti aleiha) suggests that even the specific judgments against other nations were designed as warnings to Jerusalem. Instead (achen hishkimu hishchitu kol alilotam) — they rose early to corrupt all their deeds. Early rising in Hebrew idiom signals eager, deliberate action (cf. Jeremiah 7:13; Zephaniah 11:7; Zephaniah 25:3 — YHWH's persistent speaking; here human eagerness for corruption).
8: 'Therefore wait for me, declares YHWH, for the day when I rise up to seize the prey. For my decision is to gather nations, to assemble kingdoms, to pour out upon them my indignation, all my burning anger; for in the fire of my jealousy all the earth shall be consumed.' The pivot from accusation to eschatological promise: the Day of YHWH against the nations is simultaneously the day of Israel's vindication. YHWH rises up as a witness (laed) or as one seizing prey (letor — the verb used of a bird of prey swooping). The universal gathering of nations for judgment in YHWH's jealousy-fire anticipates Joel 3's valley of judgment and Rev 20's great white throne.
9: 'For at that time I will change the speech of the peoples to a pure speech, that all of them may call upon the name of YHWH and serve him with one accord.' The restoration oracle that follows judgment: the reversal of Babel. At Babel, YHWH confused the speech of the nations (Genesis 11:7-9); at the eschatological restoration, YHWH gives the nations a pure lip (safah verurah) — unified language to call on his name. The universal calling on YHWH's name (liqro kulam beshem YHWH) echoes Joel 2:32 (Acts 2:21; Romans 10:13). The unity of worship across all peoples serving YHWH shoulder to shoulder (shechem echad, one shoulder/accord) is the end of Babel's division.
10: 'From beyond the rivers of Cush my worshipers, the daughter of my dispersed ones, shall bring my offering.' The furthest reaches of the known world — beyond Cush (the rivers of Cush = Nile tributaries in Nubia, the southernmost boundary of the known world) — will bring offerings to YHWH. The dispersed ones (bat putzai) are YHWH's scattered people, gathered from exile. The offering they bring is themselves — the ingathering of exiles is itself the sacrifice that YHWH receives.
11: 'On that day you shall not be put to shame because of the deeds by which you have rebelled against me; for then I will remove from your midst your proudly exultant ones, and you shall no longer be haughty in my holy mountain.' The shame of the exile removed. The specific condition for restored dignity: the removal of the proudly exultant ones (alyzei ga'avatech, those exulting in your pride) from the midst of Jerusalem. The pride that caused the fall is surgically removed; what remains is a humble community that does not boast on YHWH's holy mountain.
12: 'But I will leave in your midst a people humble and lowly. They shall seek refuge in the name of YHWH.' The remnant (she'erit) theology reaches its clearest expression: the remnant is defined not by ethnic purity or national affiliation but by character — humble (ani vedalh, poor and afflicted) and those who seek refuge in YHWH's name. This is the Beatitude pattern before the Beatitudes: the poor in spirit who inherit the kingdom (Matthew 5:3). The name of YHWH is the refuge, not the walls of Jerusalem or the political alliances that the princes pursued.
13: 'The remnant of Israel; they shall do no injustice and speak no lies, nor shall there be found in their mouth a deceitful tongue. For they shall graze and lie down, and none shall make them afraid.' The remnant's character: no injustice, no lies, no deceit — the exact vices of the oppressing city described in Zephaniah 3:1-4. The restoration reverses the corruption. The pastoral image of lying down in safety (none to make them afraid) directly echoes Micah 4:4 (every man under his vine and fig tree, none to make them afraid) and Ezekiel 34:28 (the good shepherd's flock at rest). The covenant blessings of Leviticus 26:6 and Deut 28 are fulfilled.
14: 'Sing aloud, O daughter of Zion; shout, O Israel! Rejoice and exult with all your heart, O daughter of Jerusalem!' The sudden turn to celebration is one of the most dramatic pivots in the entire prophetic corpus. After fifteen verses of cosmic wrath (Zephaniah 1) and the detailed judgment of nations and Jerusalem (Zephaniah 2-Zephaniah 3:1-13), the prophet breaks into an imperative of joy. Four verbs of celebration in two verses: sing, shout, rejoice, exult. The daughter of Zion is addressed as the one who will receive the restored divine presence.
15: 'YHWH has taken away the judgments against you; he has cleared away your enemies. The King of Israel, YHWH, is in your midst; you shall never again fear evil.' The three gifts of restoration: removal of judgments (hitir mishpatayich), clearing of enemies (pinna oyevaich), and the divine King's presence (melech Yisrael YHWH bekerevech). YHWH as King of Israel in the midst of his people is the fulfillment of the Sinai covenant's central promise (Leviticus 26:11-12; Exodus 29:45-46). 'You shall never again fear evil' — the exact reversal of the Day of YHWH's terror announced in Zephaniah 1.
16: 'On that day it shall be said to Jerusalem: Fear not, O Zion; let not your hands grow weak.' The encouragement formula 'Fear not' (al tiri) is the angel's greeting to those who receive divine appearances (Genesis 15:1; Zephaniah 26:24; Daniel 10:12; Luke 1:13, 30; Zephaniah 2:10). Weak hands (al yirpu yadayich, do not let your hands go slack) is the image of discouragement and helplessness (2 Samuel 4:1; Isaiah 35:3; Hebrews 12:12). The restoration makes Jerusalem capable of action — her hands are strengthened.
17: 'YHWH your God is in your midst, a mighty one who will save; he will rejoice over you with gladness; he will quiet you by his love; he will exult over you with loud singing.' One of the most remarkable verses in the OT: YHWH himself sings over his people. The divine warrior (gibbor yoshi'a, mighty one who saves) is simultaneously the singer. YHWH rejoices (yasis) and exults (yagil) — the same verbs used of Israel's commanded rejoicing in Zephaniah 3:14, now attributed to YHWH himself. The quieting in love (yacharish be'ahavato) — the silence of deep contentment and satisfaction. The image of YHWH singing over restored Jerusalem is without parallel in the prophetic corpus.
18: 'I will gather those of you who mourn for the festival, so that you will no longer suffer reproach.' The gathering of those who mourned for the festivals (nugei mo'ed asafti mimcha hayu) — those exiled who could not attend Jerusalem's pilgrimage festivals and mourned their absence. The Psalms of Ascent (Pss 120-134) express this longing. The reproach (cherpa) of exile — the shame of deportation, the mockery of enemies — is removed in the gathering.
19: 'Behold, at that time I will deal with all your oppressors. And I will save the lame and gather the outcast, and I will change their shame into praise and renown in all the earth.' The lame (hatzoleat) and the outcast (hanidachah) — those on the margins of society who were excluded from full participation in communal life — are specifically gathered. The same categories appear in Micah 4:6-7: YHWH will gather the lame and the driven away. The shame-to-praise reversal is the eschatological transformation of status: those who were last become known throughout all the earth.
20: 'At that time I will bring you in, at the time when I gather you together; for I will make you renowned and praised among all the peoples of the earth, when I restore your fortunes before your eyes, says YHWH.' The final verse of Zephaniah closes with the promise that spans the entire book: from the universal judgment of Zephaniah 1 to the universal restoration of Zephaniah 3. The restoration of fortunes (beshuvi et shevuteichem) is a standard post-exilic phrase for the reversal of exile (Deuteronomy 30:3; Jeremiah 30:3; Joel 4:1; Amos 9:14). The final clause — 'before your eyes, says YHWH' — grounds the promise in the immediacy of divine speech addressed to the very people who will see it fulfilled.
